description: Figure 2 presents scanning electron microscopy (SEM) micrographs showing the microstructural evolution of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels (LDSs) under different solution treatment conditions. The caption identifies panel (a) as S0 in the as-hot-rolled state without solution treatment, displaying a nonuniform, deformed microstructure with fragmented grains. Panels (b), (c), and (d) correspond to samples S950, S1050, and S1150 after solution treatment at 950 °C, 1050 °C, and 1150 °C for 2 h with water quenching, respectively. Visually, the images reveal a progressive transition from fine equiaxed austenitic grains with abundant annealing twin boundaries in S950 to intermediate-sized grains in S1050 and coarse equiaxed grains in S1150, accompanied by a marked reduction in twin boundary density and grain boundary area as grain coarsening occurs. Published measurements report average grain sizes of approximately 8.7 μm for S950 and 32.0 μm for S1150. All solution-treated samples exhibit fully recrystallized microstructures, contrasting with the retained deformation structure in S0. This systematic grain size and grain boundary density variation establishes the microstructural foundation supporting the core conclusion that fine-grained structures promote dense, Al-rich oxide film formation and thereby enhance corrosion resistance.
